content:
# Genesis Chapter 22 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12r–12v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_022_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_022_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 17–18 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 12r–12v, West Semitic Research Project, © Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 12r (GEN_022_page_001.jpg) opens the chapter at the petuchah paragraph marker visible mid-column, immediately following the close of Genesis 21, and carries the Aqedah narrative through approximately verse 13–14 in the characteristic three-column Tiberian Masoretic layout with full niqqud (vowel points) and te'amim (cantillation marks). Folio 12v (GEN_022_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 22:14–24 — the divine naming, the second angelic oracle, the renewed oath and blessing, the return to Beer-sheba, and the Nahor genealogy closing with Rebekah. The folio 12v image is reproduced in inverted orientation; the Hebrew text is fully legible once corrected. The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana) pages 17–18 have been read directly: page 17 (pa_0021) carries the Aqedah narrative in continuous uncial script; page 18 (pa_0022) carries the latter section through the genealogy and into Genesis 23, with a chapter marker "CAP. XXV" visible at the top of the page in the ancient Greek enumeration and a marginal "CAP. IIII" notation at the right column. The Dead Sea Scroll fragment 4Q225 (Pseudo-Jubilees), which contains an interpretive retelling of the Aqedah with expansions including a role for the adversary figure Mastema, is noted as a Second Temple period witness but is not used as a source for this canonical translation.

---

*The Aqedah — The Testing of Abraham (vv. 1–14)*

**22:1** After these things God tested [*nissah* — נִסָּה] Abraham and said to him, "Abraham!" And he said, "Here I am."

**22:2** He said, "Take your son — your only son [*yechidkha* — יְחִידְךָ] — whom you love — Isaac — and go to the land of Moriah [הָאֱלֹהִים — *ha-Moriyyah*], and offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains of which I shall tell you."

**22:3** And Abraham rose early in the morning and saddled his donkey and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son, and he split the wood for the burnt offering. Then he arose and went to the place of which God had told him.

**22:4** On the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes and saw the place from afar.

**22:5** Then Abraham said to his young men, "Stay here with the donkey. The boy and I will go over there and worship, and we will come back to you."

**22:6** And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son. He himself took in his hand the fire and the knife. And the two of them walked on together.

**22:7** And Isaac said to Abraham his father, "My father!" And he said, "Here I am, my son." He said, "Look — the fire and the wood — but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?"

**22:8** And Abraham said, "God will provide for himself [*yir'eh-lo* — יִרְאֶה-לוֹ] the lamb for the burnt offering, my son." And the two of them walked on together.

**22:9** When they came to the place of which God had told him, Abraham built the altar there and arranged the wood. And he bound [*wayyaʿăqōd* — וַיַּעֲקֹד] Isaac his son and laid him on the altar, on top of the wood.

**22:10** Then Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son.

**22:11** But the angel of the LORD called to him from the heavens and said, "Abraham! Abraham!" And he said, "Here I am."

**22:12** He said, "Do not lay your hand on the boy, and do nothing to him — for now I know [*kî ʿattāh yādaʿtî* — כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי] that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son — your only son — from me."

**22:13** And Abraham lifted up his eyes and saw — and look — a ram, behind [*ʾachar* — אַחַר], caught in the thicket by its horns. And Abraham went and took the ram and offered it up as a burnt offering in place of his son. ⚠️

**22:14** And Abraham called the name of that place "YHWH-yireh" [יְהוָה יִרְאֶה — *the LORD will see / the LORD will provide*] — as it is said to this day: "On the mount of the LORD it shall be seen." ⚠️

---

*The Renewed Oath and Blessing (vv. 15–18)*

**22:15** And the angel of the LORD called to Abraham a second time from the heavens

**22:16** and said: "By myself I have sworn — declares the LORD — because you have done this thing and have not withheld your son — your only son —

**22:17** I will surely bless you, and I will surely multiply your offspring as the stars of the heavens and as the sand that is on the shore of the sea. And your offspring shall possess the gate of his enemies.

**22:18** And in your offspring all the nations of the earth shall be blessed [*hitbarekhu* — הִתְבָּרֲכוּ] — because you have listened to my voice." ⚠️

---

*Return to Beer-sheba (v. 19)*

**22:19** So Abraham returned to his young men, and they arose and went together to Beer-sheba. And Abraham dwelt at Beer-sheba.

---

*The Genealogy of Nahor — Rebekah Introduced (vv. 20–24)*

**22:20** Now after these things it was told to Abraham: "Behold, Milcah has also borne children to your brother Nahor:

**22:21** Uz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram,

**22:22** and Chesed and Hazo and Pildash and Jidlaph and Bethuel."

**22:23** And Bethuel fathered Rebekah. These eight Milcah bore to Nahor, the brother of Abraham.

**22:24** And his concubine — whose name was Reumah — she also bore Tebah and Gaham and Tahash and Maacah.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the Architecture of Genesis 22 — Three Movements

All three council translators identified the same three narrative movements in this chapter, and the council adopts their shared structural analysis:

1. **vv. 1–14** — The Aqedah: the command, the journey, the binding, the raising of the knife, the angel's intervention, the ram, and the great naming.
2. **vv. 15–19** — The renewed oath: the divine self-oath (*bî nišbaʿtî*), the sworn blessing and promise of innumerable offspring, and the return to Beer-sheba.
3. **vv. 20–24** — The genealogical coda: the family of Nahor, culminating in the first naming of Rebekah.

The council adopts LOGOS's architectural observation as its governing frame: the chapter is structured around two governing verbs — *halakh* (הָלַךְ, "to go/walk"), which dominates the test section (the journey to the mountain, expressed most pointedly in the refrain *"they walked together,"* vv. 6, 8), and *ra'ah* (רָאָה, "to see/provide"), which dominates the resolution section (Abraham's faith-statement in v. 8, the place naming in v. 14, and the etiological proverb). The chapter's movement is from walking toward an impossible thing to seeing that what God provides was already there.

PNEUMA's observation on the chapter's governing question is also adopted: the narrative does not ask whether Abraham will obey — verse 3 makes clear he will — but what kind of God demands and then provides, commands and then cancels. The chapter is its own answer, concentrated in the name *YHWH-yireh*.

The council further notes the structural framing of *"the two of them walked together"* (*vayyelekhu shenehem yachdav*) as a literary and theological marker. It appears at the end of verse 6 (before the question about the lamb) and again at the end of verse 8 (after the answer about provision) — the same two figures, still walking side by side, despite the knife. PNEUMA's note is adopted: the repetition is the genius of the passage. After the question has been asked and the knife has in some sense been named between them, they still walk together.

---

### On 22:1 — "God tested Abraham" — *nissah* (נִסָּה)

All three translators agreed on "tested" for *nissah* (נִסָּה, Piel of נ-ס-ה) and the council follows unanimously (3–0). EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all independently documented: (a) the Piel *nissah* means to test, prove, or put to trial — never "to tempt" in the sense of enticing to evil; (b) the same root forms the noun *massah* (מַסָּה) at Exod 17:7 for the site where Israel tested God; (c) the narrator's disclosure of the test at the opening is a deliberate act of narrative grace — the reader knows what Abraham does not, and this controls the entire reading of what follows; (d) the LXX Vaticanus uses *epeiradzen* (ἐπείραζεν), the imperfect of *peirazō* (πειράζω), which can mean "to tempt" in later Greek — a semantically fraught equivalence addressed in James 1:13. The council renders "tested" throughout and retains the opening narrator's disclosure without alteration.

LOGOS's note on the article *ha-'Elohim* (הָאֱלֹהִים) — "the God" — appearing with unusual frequency throughout the Aqedah (vv. 1, 3, 8, 9, 12) as a marker of this narrative's solemn register is adopted as a council observation. The formal translation renders it simply "God" in the translation text, as the definite article before *Elohim* is regularly suppressed in English usage.

PNEUMA's note on the phrase "after all this" (*ʾachar haddevarim hāʾēlleh*, אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה) is adopted: this is a standard Genesis transitional formula, but here it carries enormous weight. After the long wait for Isaac, the joy of his birth, the painful expulsion of Ishmael, and the settled contentment of chapter 21 (Abraham planting a tamarisk, calling on the Everlasting God, dwelling at Beer-sheba) — after all this — comes the test. The phrase signals that everything comfortable has just ended.

---

### On 22:2 — The fourfold identification of Isaac — *yechidkha* (יְחִידְךָ)

All three translators preserved all four elements of the divine command's intensifying identification — "your son... your only son... whom you love... Isaac" — and the council follows unanimously (3–0). The fourfold structure is the Hebrew text's own, and its devastating deliberateness must not be reduced.

The council adopts EMET's note on the first appearance of "love" (*ʾāhab*, אָהַב) in the Bible: this is the word's debut in scripture, and it is a father's love for his son, commanded to be surrendered. The sequence of intensification — each element making the demand harder until the name lands at the end — is the text's rhetorical achievement.

The council adopts LOGOS's presentation of the rendering order: "your son — your only son — whom you love — Isaac," with the name in penultimate position exactly as in the Hebrew rather than rearranged.

On *yechidkha* (יְחִידְךָ): the council rules 3–0 for "your only son," following the Hebrew *yāchîd* (יָחִיד, "only / unique / solitary"), over against the LXX Vaticanus rendering *agapētos* (ἀγαπητός, "beloved"). The LXX's interpretive translation — substituting "beloved" for "only" — is theologically consequential: the identical Greek word appears at Jesus' baptism ("This is my beloved Son," Matt 3:17; Mark 1:11; Luke 3:22) and Transfiguration, creating a verbal bridge between the Aqedah and the Gospel accounts of the divine voice. The council fully acknowledges this LXX trajectory (documented in the Disagreement Log) but follows the Masoretic Hebrew of the Leningrad Codex. The covenantal and legal specificity of *yāchîd* — Isaac is the only son of promise, the one through whom the covenant continues — carries a distinct theological force that "beloved" does not precisely replicate. Both EMET and LOGOS argued for "only son" against the LXX; PNEUMA's dynamic rendering follows the same conclusion.

The council preserves "the land of Moriah" (*ʾerets ha-Moriyyah*, אֶרֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּה) as a transliterated place name without etymological glossing in the translation text. EMET's note documenting the three competing etymologies — from *raʾah* ("to see"), *yārēʾ* ("to fear"), and *yārah* ("to teach/instruct") — is adopted in full. The connection to Solomon's Temple (2 Chr 3:1: "Solomon began to build the house of the LORD in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah") is noted as the canonical identification of this site. The council adopts the transliterated form "Moriah" without attempting to resolve the etymology in the text.

The particle *nāʾ* (נָא, "please") before "take" (*qach-nāʾ*, קַח-נָא): PNEUMA's note is adopted — this particle of polite entreaty is remarkable in the context of a divine command. God does not simply order; the particle signals a request made without coercion. LOGOS and EMET also noted its presence. The council renders it as part of the natural-English "take" without making the entreaty explicit (which would require "please take" — potentially miscued in English), following LOGOS's formal rendering, while noting its presence here.

---

### On 22:3 — The wordless obedience — six verbs of action

All three translators preserved every verb in verse 3 and the council follows (3–0). EMET's note on the sequence of six consecutive action verbs — *wayyaškem... wayyachăbōš... wayyiqqach... wayyĕbaqqĕaʿ... wayyāqom... wayyēlek* ("and he rose early... and he saddled... and he took... and he split... and he arose... and he went") — with no speech, no delay, no hesitation, and no recorded interior response is adopted as the council's governing observation on this verse. The rapid verbal sequence is itself a theological statement: obedience that does not pause to deliberate or mourn is the form Abraham's trust takes.

LOGOS's note that the idiom "rose early in the morning" (*wayyaškem babbōqer*, וַיַּשְׁכֵּם בַּבֹּקֶר) appears also at Genesis 21:14 — Abraham's early rising to send Hagar away — is adopted. Both are immediate responses to painful divine commands; both are marked by the same vocabulary of urgent, resolute obedience.

---

### On 22:4 — "Lifted up his eyes and saw"

All three translators agreed on "lifted up his eyes and saw" — the literal Hebrew idiom *wayyissāʾ... ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarʾ* (וַיִּשָּׂא... אֶת-עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא). The council follows unanimously. LOGOS's instruction per LOGOS's own guidelines — "do not write 'he looked up'" when the text says "he lifted his eyes" — is adopted as the council's standard. The idiom is fully intelligible in English and must not be smoothed into a more colloquial equivalent. The council notes that the same idiom recurs at verse 13 when Abraham sees the ram, binding the two moments of sight together.

---

### On 22:5 — "We will come back to you" — first-person plural verbs

All three translators preserved the first-person plural verbs and the council follows unanimously (3–0): *nēlĕkāh* ("we will go"), *wĕništachăweh* ("we will worship"), *wĕnāšûbāh* ("we will come back/return") — all cover both Abraham and Isaac as grammatical subjects.

The council's consensus rendering: "The boy and I will go over there and worship, and we will come back to you."

The interpretive question — whether Abraham is deceiving his servants, speaking in genuine faith that God will provide a substitute, or speaking in the logic of trust in divine resurrection (so Heb 11:19) — is left open by the text and the council follows the text. PNEUMA's note is adopted: the plural held. At verse 19, after everything, Abraham returns — to his servants, with Isaac — and the plural *nāšûbāh* ("we will return") proved true. The council records this as a genuine textual ambiguity: the Hebrew grammar cannot resolve whether Abraham knew, hoped, or simply trusted, and no translation should impose a resolution the text refuses to give.

---

### On 22:6 — "The fire and the knife" / "they walked together"

All three translators agreed on the key elements. The council follows the LOGOS rendering for the overall verse structure: the wood on Isaac, Abraham carrying fire and the knife, and the concluding refrain "the two of them walked on together."

The council adopts EMET's note on *maʾăkelet* (מַאֲכֶלֶת, "knife/slaughtering knife"): the word derives from *ʾākal* (אָכַל, "to eat/consume"), making it literally "the eating-thing" — a large slaughter knife. The LXX uses *machaira* (μάχαιρα), a more general term. The council renders "knife" in the translation text, noting the etymological texture here rather than forcing it into the English.

---

### On 22:7–8 — Isaac's question and Abraham's answer — *yirʾeh-lo* (יִרְאֶה-לוֹ)

All three translators agreed on Isaac's question ("Where is the lamb for the burnt offering?") and the council follows.

On Abraham's answer — *ʾElōhîm yirʾeh-lô haśśeh laʿōlāh* (אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה-לוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה) — all three translators arrived at "God will provide" as the functional English equivalent of *yirʾeh* ("will see"), while preserving *lo* ("for himself") as the council's rendering, following EMET (3–0 on the *lo*).

The council's rendering: "God will provide for himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son."

This rendering was chosen over PNEUMA's "God will provide the lamb" (which drops *lo*) because *lo* ("for himself") is present in the Hebrew and carries the reflexive-dative force that connects directly to the naming of the place in verse 14 (*YHWH-yireh* — "the LORD will see/provide") and to the LXX Vaticanus's *heautō* (ἑαυτῷ, "for himself"), which faithfully preserves the reflexive. The council adopts EMET's note: the provision is entirely God's initiative, from within his own resources. LOGOS's note is also adopted: "for himself" is not redundant but signals this theological claim.

The council adopts EMET's broader note on the theology of *yirʾeh*: the verb *rāʾāh* ("to see") governs the entire naming theology of the chapter — God sees the lamb (v. 8), Abraham names the place *YHWH-yireh* ("the LORD will see/provide," v. 14), and the etiological proverb states "on the mount of the LORD it shall be seen" (v. 14, Niphal passive). To see and to provide are the same act in this chapter's theology.

PNEUMA's note on the phrase's double function — Abraham gives Isaac a true answer without yet fully knowing how the answer will be fulfilled — is adopted as a council observation. The statement operates simultaneously as faith-statement and as literal truth that the narrative goes on to vindicate.

---

### On 22:9 — "He bound" (*wayyaʿăqōd*) — the Aqedah

All three translators agreed on "bound" for *wayyaʿăqōd* (וַיַּעֲקֹד) and the council follows unanimously (3–0). EMET's note on the hapax status of *ʿāqad* (עָקַד) is adopted in full: this verb — used only here in the entire Hebrew Bible — means specifically to bind the legs (as one ties an animal for slaughter). The entire episode, the entire tradition, carries the name *ha-ʿAqedāh* (הָעֲקֵדָה, "the Binding") from this single, unrepeated word. The LXX Vaticanus renders it *edesen* (ἔδησεν, "bound/tied"), a more general term. The council renders "bound" as the closest English equivalent that carries both the binding action and its terrible specificity without technical jargon.

The council adopts the observation from both LOGOS and PNEUMA that Isaac is silent throughout the binding and the preparation. The text says nothing of protest or struggle. The silence around Isaac — his inner state withheld from the reader entirely — is among the most theologically resonant silences in scripture.

---

### On 22:10 — "Stretched out his hand"

All three translators agreed in substance. The council follows EMET's rendering "stretched out his hand" (*wayyišlach ʾAvrāhām ʾet-yādō*, וַיִּשְׁלַח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יָדוֹ) over LOGOS's "reached out his hand" — preserving the specific Hebrew verb *šālach* (שָׁלַח, "to stretch out / send forth"), which will recur in verse 12 ("do not stretch out your hand"). The verbal echo between v. 10 and v. 12 — the same verb for the action arrested — is a deliberate literary hinge. LOGOS and PNEUMA noted this connection; the council ensures the translation preserves it.

---

### On 22:11 — "Abraham! Abraham!" — the doubled name

All three translators preserved the doubled name and the council follows unanimously. EMET's note on the significance of the doubling is adopted: the repeated name signals urgency, intimacy, and a stay of execution. The same doubling appears at critical divine addresses: "Moses! Moses!" (Exod 3:4), "Samuel! Samuel!" (1 Sam 3:10), "Saul! Saul!" (Acts 9:4). The single "Abraham!" of verse 1 contrasts with the doubled "Abraham! Abraham!" of verse 11. PNEUMA's note is adopted: at verse 1 the call opens the test; at verse 11 the call ends it. Both are answered with the same word: *hinnēnî* ("Here I am").

PNEUMA's observation on *hinnēnî* (הִנֵּנִי, "Here I am") is adopted as a council note: Abraham uses this same word at verse 1, verse 7 (answering Isaac), and verse 11. He is consistently and completely present — available to God, to his son, and to the angel. The single word is his entire interior monologue throughout the chapter.

---

### On 22:12 — "Now I know" — divine epistemology

All three translators rendered "now I know" and the council follows (3–0). The theological question this phrase raises — does the omniscient God learn something new? — is left open by the text, and the council's rendering preserves that openness without harmonization or resolution.

The council adopts EMET's note: the word *ʿattāh* (עַתָּה, "now") is emphatic. The moment of the raised knife is the moment of the divine knowing. Whether this reflects a genuine epistemological moment for God or a narrative and rhetorical idiom for divine confirmation in time — the council declines to adjudicate. The literal translator's task is to render the text as it stands; the theologian's task begins there.

LOGOS's note on "you fear God" (*yĕrēʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh*, יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה) is adopted: the fear of God is the integrating virtue of the wisdom tradition (Prov 9:10), the central virtue of Genesis 20 (Abimelech's question), and the character of Job (Job 1:1). Here Abraham is formally certified by heaven as one who fears God. The rendering "you fear God" (verbal phrase) is preferred over "you are a God-fearer" (nominal phrase) to preserve the active and relational character of the Hebrew.

The council notes that the LXX Vaticanus reads *nun gar egnōn* (νῦν γὰρ ἔγνων, "for now I have known"), adding a connective particle *gar* ("for") not in the Hebrew. The Hebrew *kî* in *kî ʿattāh yādaʿtî* functions asseveratively ("indeed now I know") rather than as a causal particle; the council follows the Hebrew.

---

### On 22:13 — "A ram, behind" (*ʾayil ʾachar*) — the textual variant ⚠️

See Disagreement Log below for the full discussion of *ʾachar* ("behind") vs. *ʾechad* ("one").

The council rules to follow the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex: "a ram, behind, caught in the thicket by its horns."

---

### On 22:14 — YHWH-yireh and the etiological proverb ⚠️

All three translators agreed on the place name "YHWH-yireh" and the council follows, retaining the Hebrew name untranslated in the text (as a proper name) with the gloss given in the council's notes.

See Disagreement Log below for the full discussion of "it shall be seen" vs. "it shall be provided."

---

### On 22:15–16 — "By myself I have sworn" — the divine self-oath

All three translators agreed on "By myself I have sworn — declares the LORD" and the council follows unanimously. EMET's note on *bî nišbaʿtî* (בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי, "by myself I have sworn") is adopted: God swears by God's own name because there is no greater by which God can swear (cf. Heb 6:13). This divine self-oath is theologically unprecedented in the Abraham cycle — earlier promises (Gen 12:1–3; 15:1–21) were given as covenant-with-sign; this is a sworn, unconditional, irrevocable oath produced by Abraham's obedience in the Aqedah.

EMET's note on *nĕʾum-YHWH* (נְאֻם-יְהוָה, "declares the LORD" / "oracle of the LORD") is adopted: this formula, which accompanies prophetic speech throughout the OT, appears here uniquely in Genesis — further elevating the gravity of vv. 16–18 above all prior divine speech to Abraham.

---

### On 22:17 — "I will surely bless you" — the infinitive absolute construction

All three translators agreed on "I will surely bless you" and "I will surely multiply your offspring" for the Hebrew infinitive absolute constructions (*bārēk ʾăbārĕkĕkā*, כִּי-בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ; *wĕharbēh ʾarbeh*, וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה). The council follows unanimously. LOGOS's note is adopted: the doubled form (infinitive absolute + finite verb of the same root) is the standard Hebrew emphatic construction — "I will certainly / I will indeed" — and "I will surely" is the most natural English equivalent that carries the emphatic force without the awkwardness of a literal "blessing I will bless."

On "your offspring shall possess the gate of his enemies" (*yîraš zarʿăkā ʾēt šaʿar ʾōyĕbāyw*, וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו): all three translators preserved the idiom literally, and the council follows. LOGOS's note is adopted: "possessing the gate" of one's enemies is a military and civic idiom for complete control of a city, since the gate was the seat of legal, commercial, and military power. To render it "defeat your enemies" (as some dynamic translations do) would erase the specific and vivid imagery of the Hebrew. The council preserves "the gate of his enemies" — singular "gate" functioning as a collective, with the singular/plural shift in *ʾōyĕbāyw* ("his enemies") preserved as a feature of Hebrew *zeraʿ* (seed/offspring) as a collective noun.

---

### On 22:18 — *hitbarekhu* — reflexive or passive? ⚠️

See Disagreement Log below for the full adjudication. The council rules for "shall be blessed" following the dominant interpretive tradition and the LXX, with the Hithpael ambiguity fully documented.

---

### On 22:19 — "Abraham returned to his young men" — Isaac unnamed

All three translators preserved the Hebrew as it stands and the council follows. EMET's note is adopted as the council's governing observation: the most haunting textual silence in the chapter. After the Aqedah, the narrative says "Abraham returned to his young men and they arose and walked together to Beer-sheba and Abraham dwelt at Beer-sheba." Isaac is not named. He appears as the subject of no verb. The narrative began with "they walked together" (vv. 6, 8) and ends with "they walked together" (v. 19) — but now the pronoun refers to Abraham and his servants, not Abraham and Isaac. Whether Isaac was present throughout the journey back (the natural assumption) or whether the text's silence signals something about the space that opened between father and son after the mountain, is a genuine and irreducible textual gap.

PNEUMA's observation is adopted: Genesis will not tell us what they said on the walk down. What the text will tell us, three chapters later, is that at Sarah's death Isaac is living separately from Abraham. Whether the Aqedah fractured something between them is not said — but the text remembers the distance.

The council renders the verse as the Hebrew gives it, without harmonizing or filling the silence.

---

### On 22:20–24 — The genealogy of Nahor — Rebekah introduced

All three translators agreed in substance on the genealogy and the council follows, adopting the LOGOS/EMET rendering of individual names as the canonical English forms (Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, Bethuel, Rebekah, Reumah, Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, Maacah).

The council adopts LOGOS's note on the structural function of the genealogy: the chapter that opens with the most extreme threat to the line of promise closes with the quiet assurance that the line continues. The introduction of Rebekah here — named for the first time in verse 23, the last name in the list of Milcah's children — is structurally preparatory for Genesis 24. PNEUMA's note is adopted as the council's closing observation: she appears with no drama and no description, one name in a list, but the reader of Genesis knows from narrative momentum that this name matters. The genealogy is not a postscript. It is a preview.

The council adopts EMET's note on "concubine" (*pîlegesh*, פִּילֶגֶשׁ) as a legal category below the primary wife (*ʾiššāh*) but above a slave woman (*ʾāmāh*). The standard rendering "concubine" is followed unanimously.

EMET's observation on the twelve descendants of Nahor — eight sons of Milcah and four sons of Reumah — paralleling the twelve sons of Ishmael (Gen 25:12–16) and anticipating the twelve tribes of Israel is adopted as a council note.

---

### On the Governing Theological Claim of Genesis 22

The council adopts the following as its combined summary from all three translators:

Genesis 22 is the chapter in which the fundamental tension of the Abrahamic narrative is fully exposed: God commanded "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" (21:12) and now commands "take Isaac and offer him." These two divine words are in direct contradiction, and Abraham walks three days into that contradiction without resolving it intellectually. He resolves it by action — the foot set on the mountain, the stone laid on stone, the wood arranged, the son bound, the knife drawn.

The chapter's answer to its own question is not the ram (though the ram is what saves Isaac's life). The answer is the name: *YHWH-yireh*. The LORD sees. The LORD provides. On the mountain of the LORD, it will be seen. The seeing and the providing are the same act — divine attention is divine provision. The place where God demanded and then provided, where God commanded and then stayed the hand, is the place where *seeing* became the theological name for everything God does.

All three council translators — EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — arrived independently at this reading. The council records it as the chapter's own answer, concentrated in nine Hebrew letters: *YHWH-yireh*.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### 22:2 — *yechidkha* (יְחִידְךָ): "your only son" or "your beloved son"?

**The Question:** The Hebrew *yāchîd* (יָחִיד) means "only / unique / solitary." The LXX Vaticanus renders it *agapētos* (ἀγαπητός, "beloved"). Which rendering is correct for the consensus translation?

**EMET's Position:** "Your only son" — following the Hebrew *yāchîd*. Argued that *yāchîd* carries a precise legal and covenantal force (Isaac is the only son of promise, the one through whom the covenant continues) that "beloved" does not replicate. Noted the LXX's *agapētos* as an interpretive shift. Documented the NT trajectory: Paul's language in Rom 8:32 ("did not spare his own Son") echoes Gen 22:16; the Synoptic *agapētos* at Jesus' baptism draws on the LXX. Retained "only son."

**LOGOS's Position:** "Your only son" — explicitly following MT over LXX. Documented at length the LXX rendering *agapētos* and its significance for the baptism/Transfiguration accounts (Matt 3:17; 17:5; Mark 1:11; Luke 3:22). Argued that the formal translation follows the Masoretic Hebrew, which is the primary source, and noted the LXX's interpretive translation as a secondary trajectory. The verbal bridge between the Aqedah's LXX and the Gospel divine voice ("my beloved Son") is acknowledged as "unmistakable and theologically freighted" — but LOGOS follows MT. Retained "only son."

**PNEUMA's Position:** Used "your only son, Isaac, whom you love" — following MT, letting the word "love" (which immediately follows in the Hebrew) carry the relational-affective force that the LXX captured with *agapētos*. Did not use "beloved" but implicitly acknowledged why the LXX went there. Retained "only son."

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **3–0 for "your only son"** — unanimous. All three translators follow the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex.

The LXX Vaticanus rendering *agapētos* ("beloved") represents one of the most theologically consequential translation decisions in the Septuagint's history. By substituting "beloved" for "only," the LXX created a verbal bridge that would be activated in the Gospel accounts of the divine voice at Jesus' baptism and Transfiguration. Whether this was deliberate typological interpretation or a felicitous translation judgment, the resonance between the "beloved son" Isaac (wood on shoulders, ascending the mountain) and the "beloved Son" Jesus (cross on shoulders, ascending to execution) is unmistakable in the Christian reading tradition. The council records this as a textual variant of the highest theological significance while following the primary Hebrew source.

Concretely: the LXX says *agapētos* ("beloved"); the Hebrew says *yāchîd* ("only"). Both are true of Isaac; both are true of Jesus in Christian theology. The Hebrew privileges the covenantal uniqueness; the Greek privileges the affective bond. The council preserves the Hebrew's covenantal precision and documents the LXX's interpretive tradition here.

---

### 22:8 — *yirʾeh-lo* (יִרְאֶה-לוֹ): "provide for himself" vs. "provide" vs. "see to it"

**The Question:** How literally should the reflexive-dative *lo* (לוֹ, "for himself") be rendered in Abraham's answer to Isaac?

**EMET's Position:** "God will provide for himself the lamb for the burnt offering." Retained *lo* ("for himself") explicitly, arguing that the dative of interest connects directly to the place name *YHWH-yireh* in verse 14 and the LXX's *heautō* ("for himself"). The verb *yirʾeh* means "will see" — transliterated *yir'eh-lo* in the translation text.

**LOGOS's Position:** "God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering." Identical conclusion. Documented the LXX's *heautō* ("for himself") as confirming the reflexive. Argued explicitly that "for himself" is not redundant but signals that the provision is entirely God's initiative from within God's own resources. Noted that NIV's "God himself will provide" distributes *lo*'s force onto the subject rather than the provision.

**PNEUMA's Position:** "God will provide the lamb for the burnt offering, my son." Dropped *lo* ("for himself") in favor of a cleaner dynamic rendering. Noted the theological significance of *yirʾeh* ("will see") and argued that "provide" carries the practical sense, while acknowledging that the Hebrew "see" and English "provide" do not fully overlap. Rendered it without the reflexive.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **2–1 for "God will provide for himself the lamb"** — EMET and LOGOS over PNEUMA. The retention of "for himself" (*lo*) is theologically and textually warranted:

1. The LXX Vaticanus reads *heautō* (ἑαυτῷ, "for himself"), confirming that the ancient Greek translators understood the reflexive force.
2. The verbal and dative connection between verse 8 (*yirʾeh-lo*) and verse 14 (*YHWH-yireh*) is a structural link that the chapter itself invites: Abraham's statement about what God will do becomes the name of the place where God did it.
3. "For himself" signals that the provision is not something Abraham arranges or discovers but something God has already prepared from within his own initiative — a distinction the chapter confirms when the ram proves to have been already there.

PNEUMA's rendering "God will provide the lamb" is a clean and honorable dynamic equivalent that serves readers well; the council only parts from it on the specific point of *lo*, which carries too much structural weight in this chapter to be silently dropped.

---

### 22:12 — "Now I know" — divine epistemology

**The Question:** Does the phrase *kî ʿattāh yādaʿtî* ("for now I know") imply that God did not know before? Should the translation harmonize with divine omniscience?

**EMET's Position:** Rendered literally: "for now I know." Declined to harmonize. Noted the emphatic *ʿattāh* ("now") and that the moment of the raised knife is the moment of the divine knowing. Documented the commentarial tradition from Rashi to Kierkegaard wrestling with this. Rendered without resolution.

**LOGOS's Position:** Rendered: "for now I know." Followed the text as it stands without theological resolution. Documented classical theological responses (the "now" signals the moment knowledge becomes manifest in time, not an acquisition of new knowledge) but declined to impose them on the translation.

**PNEUMA's Position:** Rendered: "Now I know that you truly fear God." The addition of "truly" is a dynamic intensification not in the Hebrew. Otherwise consistent with EMET and LOGOS.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **2–1 for "now I know"** without the addition of "truly" — following EMET and LOGOS over PNEUMA's insertion. "Truly" has no lexical basis in the Hebrew (*ʿattāh yādaʿtî kî yĕrēʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh* contains no word for "truly") and while PNEUMA's insertion captures an appropriate emphasis, it adds to the text rather than rendering it. The emphasis is already carried by *ʿattāh* ("now") — the present moment as the moment of manifestation.

The council adopts the council's observation from EMET's notes: the question of whether God learns something new here is not for the translator to resolve. Omniscience and the "now I know" of verse 12 exist in genuine tension, and every significant theological tradition has wrestled with that tension. The council's role is to render the tension, not dissolve it.

---

### 22:13 — *ʾayil ʾachar* (אַיִל אַחַר) vs. *ʾayil ʾechad* (אַיִל אֶחָד): "behind" or "one"?

**The Question:** The Masoretic text (Leningrad Codex, folio 12r) reads *ʾachar* (אַחַר, "behind / after"), describing the spatial position of the ram relative to Abraham. But the LXX Vaticanus reads *hena* (ἕνα, "one"), corresponding to a Hebrew *Vorlage* of *ʾechad* (אֶחָד, "one"). The Samaritan Pentateuch and several Hebrew manuscripts also support *ʾechad*. Which reading should the consensus translation follow?

**EMET's Position:** Followed the Masoretic text: "a ram — behind — caught in the thicket by its horns." Defended *ʾachar* as the *lectio difficilior* (the more difficult reading, therefore generally preferred by text critics as less likely to have been a scribe's smoothing). Noted that *ʾachar* makes spatial sense: the ram was *behind* Abraham, who had been looking at Isaac on the altar — he had to turn to see it. Documented the LXX and Samaritan Pentateuch support for *ʾechad* and the plausibility of ד/ר confusion (the letters *dalet* and *resh* are easily confused in the square script). Retained *ʾachar* as the MT reading.

**LOGOS's Position:** Followed the Masoretic text: "behind him was a ram, caught in a thicket by his horns." Provided the most detailed text-critical analysis: the ד/ר confusion is well attested; the Syriac Peshitta also supports *ʾechad*; but the Leningrad Codex clearly has *ʾachar*, the formal translation follows the MT, and the spatial reading ("behind him") makes coherent narrative sense. Noted the variant explicitly in the LXX variants table.

**PNEUMA's Position:** Rendered "a ram — caught in a thicket by its horns" — dropping *ʾachar* entirely in the dynamic translation without comment. PNEUMA's notes mention the *ʾachar*/*ʾechad* variant but the translation text simply omits the word.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **2–1 to follow the Masoretic text**: "a ram, behind, caught in the thicket by its horns" — following EMET and LOGOS (with PNEUMA's evasive omission counted as neither confirming nor rejecting *ʾachar*). The council cannot follow PNEUMA's simple omission because it removes a word that is clearly present in the Leningrad Codex and whose meaning — while grammatically awkward — is coherent.

The council's resolution of the variant: the Leningrad Codex reads *ʾachar* and this is the primary source. The *lectio difficilior* principle supports it: if a scribe were going to smooth the text, smoothing *ʾachar* to *ʾechad* ("one ram" is more natural Hebrew than "a ram, behind") is more likely than the reverse. The spatial sense — Abraham had to turn around to see the ram that was there behind him — carries a theological implication that PNEUMA's notes beautifully articulate: the provision was already there, behind the horizon of Abraham's attention, while he was focused forward on the unbearable thing before him.

The council notes: "behind" translates *ʾachar* correctly. The ram was *behind* Abraham — not a directional gloss but the narrative's spatial indicator that when Abraham lifted his eyes *away* from the altar, he found what God had prepared. The seeing (*rāʾāh*) and the providing (*yirʾeh*) meet in this moment: what was already there is now seen.

---

### 22:14 — "It shall be seen" vs. "it shall be provided" — the Niphal etiological proverb

**The Question:** The verb *yērāʾeh* (יֵרָאֶה, Niphal of *rāʾāh*) in the etiological proverb "on the mount of the LORD it shall be [*yērāʾeh*]" is ambiguous in Hebrew. The Niphal can mean (a) "it shall be seen / will appear," (b) "it shall be provided" (treating the Niphal as a functional passive of the providential-Hiphil sense), or (c) "he/God shall appear" (with the LORD as implicit subject). How should the council render this?

**EMET's Position:** "On the mount of the LORD it shall be seen." Followed the literal Niphal passive, preserving the visual root *rāʾāh* and noting the full range of possible meanings. Declined to choose between "seen" and "provided."

**LOGOS's Position:** "On the mount of the LORD it shall be provided." Explicitly chose "provided" over "seen" for clarity, while acknowledging "the Hebrew verb is literally 'it shall be seen.'" Noted LXX Vaticanus reads *ōphthē* (ὤφθη, aorist passive: "the Lord appeared / was seen"), reading the Niphal as a statement of divine appearance rather than provision. Followed the providential sense as "the dominant interpretive trajectory."

**PNEUMA's Position:** "On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided." Followed the providential sense, same as LOGOS. Noted that the Niphal "opens every direction at once" — seeing, providing, appearing — and that "YHWH himself will appear" is also latent in the text. Chose "provided" for the English.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council is divided: EMET for "shall be seen"; LOGOS and PNEUMA for "shall be provided." The council rules **2–1 for "it shall be seen"** — departing from LOGOS and PNEUMA and following EMET.

The council's reasoning:

1. The verb *yērāʾeh* is a Niphal of *rāʾāh* — literally "it shall be seen" or "he/God shall appear." The word "provided" is the pragmatic English gloss for what God's *seeing* accomplishes, but it is not the word the Hebrew uses.

2. The chapter has been constructed around the root *rāʾāh* ("to see") from beginning to end. Abraham's faith-statement in verse 8 (*yirʾeh-lo*) uses it; the place name *YHWH-yireh* uses it; and now the proverb uses its Niphal passive (*yērāʾeh*). To render the third occurrence as "provided" when the first two are rendered as "see/provide" disrupts the verbal unity the chapter has been building.

3. "It shall be seen" preserves all three possible meanings simultaneously — the lamb will be seen/provided; God will appear on this mountain; whatever is needed will be shown. "It shall be provided" closes off the visionary and theophanic readings that "shall be seen" holds open.

4. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ōphthē* — the Lord "appeared" or "was seen" — which is actually *closer* to the literal "seen" than to "provided." If the LXX is a witness here, it supports "seen" over "provided."

The council acknowledges that "shall be provided" is the more transparent English communication of the pragmatic meaning and that LOGOS and PNEUMA are not wrong — only that "shall be seen" is more faithful to the Hebrew verbal architecture of this chapter as a whole.

**The place name itself:** The council retains "YHWH-yireh" in the Hebrew (untranslated as a proper name), with the following parenthetical gloss on first occurrence: "*the LORD will see / the LORD will provide*." This preserves both dimensions of the name without forcing a single English equivalent.

---

### 22:18 — *hitbarekhu* (הִתְבָּרֲכוּ) — Hithpael reflexive ("shall bless themselves") or passive ("shall be blessed")?

**The Question:** The verb *hitbarekhu* (הִתְבָּרֲכוּ, Hithpael third-person plural of *bārak*) is the reflexive-reciprocal stem. Should it be rendered "all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves [in/by your offspring]" (reflexive) or "shall be blessed" (passive)?

**EMET's Position:** Rendered "shall be blessed" — following the passive reading and the LXX/Paul tradition — but transliterated *hitbarekhu* in the translation text and documented the Hithpael ambiguity extensively. Noted the parallel at Gen 12:3 (Niphal *wĕnibrĕkû*, "shall be blessed," unambiguously passive) and that the Hithpael here introduces the reflexive possibility absent from the Niphal parallel. Noted LXX Vaticanus passive *eneulogēthēsontai* and Paul's passive reading in Gal 3:8.

**LOGOS's Position:** Rendered "shall be blessed" — following ESV, NASB, and the LXX/Paul tradition. Explicitly documented both the Hithpael reflexive possibility ("shall bless themselves") and the passive reading ("shall be blessed"). Explained that Paul in Gal 3:8 reads the passive sense and that the LXX Vaticanus resolves the ambiguity in the direction of passive blessing. Followed the passive as "the dominant interpretive trajectory."

**PNEUMA's Position:** Rendered "every nation on earth will find blessing" — a creative middle path that is "slightly softer on the passive/active distinction, letting the finding of blessing carry the weight." Neither fully passive nor fully reflexive. Noted the LXX passive and acknowledged the Hebrew holds the ambiguity.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **2–1 for "shall be blessed"** — following EMET and LOGOS over PNEUMA's creative softening. The reasons:

1. "Shall be blessed" most faithfully represents the dominant interpretive tradition rooted in the LXX Vaticanus (*eneulogēthēsontai*, passive future), which is the form Paul cites in Galatians 3:8 and on which his theological argument in Galatians 3 depends.

2. PNEUMA's rendering "will find blessing" is theologically elegant but introduces the metaphor of *finding* with no basis in the Hebrew *bārak* stem, and its softened passive/active ambiguity is a different kind of ambiguity from the Hebrew Hithpael's — it does not map transparently onto either.

3. The council acknowledges that the Hithpael form does introduce a genuine reflexive possibility: "all the nations shall bless themselves by/in your offspring" — invoking Abraham's name in blessing formulas — is a legitimate reading with ancient standing (as EMET documents). This reading would mean the nations benefit derivatively from Abraham's reputation; the passive reading means the blessing actively flows to them. The distinction is theologically significant. Paul's argument in Galatians 3 requires the passive reading: the Gentiles do not merely invoke Abraham's name but receive actual blessing through the singular "offspring" (Gal 3:16).

4. The council notes that the parallel passage at Gen 12:3 uses the Niphal (*wĕnibrĕkû*) — unambiguously passive. The shift from Niphal to Hithpael at 22:18 (and at 26:4, the other Hithpael occurrence in the Abrahamic promises) may be stylistic variation or may deliberately open the reflexive possibility. The council follows the passive rendering while fully documenting the Hithpael ambiguity here.

The council's final rendering: **"And in your offspring all the nations of the earth shall be blessed."**

---

*— SOPHIA, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
